title: The night nurse and the daughter
summary: A night-shift nurse and a daughter argue over a sedative for a dying man. Neither is wrong. The argument is about whose comfort it is.

body.speaker: Narrator
body.text: Mr Olusegun Akin is eighty-four. He is in a hospice in Manchester. He has stopped eating and is no longer fully conscious. The hospice team has offered the family the option of a stronger sedative to ease his breathing through the night. His daughter, Yetunde, has come from London. The night nurse, Hannah, is at the desk. It is shortly after midnight.

body.speaker: Yetunde
body.text: I don't want the stronger sedative. He has spent his whole life refusing painkillers. If he wakes up at three in the morning and finds himself drugged he will be angry. He used to say he wanted to die awake.

body.speaker: Hannah
body.text: I hear that. But your father's breathing tonight is not what it was yesterday. It is irregular. He is sometimes pulling in air with the muscles in his neck. That is hard work. If we do nothing extra, he will probably keep breathing like that until morning. He will not be comfortable.

body.speaker: Yetunde
body.text: Are you sure he isn't comfortable? He looks calm.

body.speaker: Hannah
body.text: He looks calm because he is too tired to look anything else. The body has a way of looking peaceful when it has run out of options. I have nursed about a hundred people through their last night. I would tell you if he were comfortable. He isn't.

body.speaker: Yetunde
body.text: But the drug will take him further away from me. He has not spoken to me for two days. If he is drugged, he won't speak to me at all.

body.speaker: Hannah
body.text: I think he will not speak to you again either way. I am sorry to be direct. It is not the drug that is taking him away.

body.speaker: Yetunde
body.text: So you are telling me I am asking him to suffer for me.

body.speaker: Hannah
body.text: I am not telling you that. You are telling yourself that, and I would gently say it is unfair to you. You came up from London. You are sitting with him. That is the opposite of selfish. But I do think it is worth asking whose comfort the choice is for.

body.speaker: Yetunde
body.text: He always said he wanted to die awake.

body.speaker: Hannah
body.text: He said that when he was well. People who have said that have, in my experience, almost always meant 'I don't want to be drugged into ten more years of half-life in a chair.' He didn't mean 'I want to drown in the last six hours.' If you ask me, that is a different sentence.

body.speaker: Hannah
body.text: Of course. I am not going anywhere. Whatever you decide, I will not think less of you. And — this matters — you can change your mind in either direction. If I give the dose and you regret it in twenty minutes, you can tell me, and I can stop. The choice is not a closing door.

body.speaker: Yetunde
body.text: Give him a small dose. Not the full one. And stay in the room with us.

body.speaker: Narrator
body.text: Mr Akin died at five past four. His breathing slowed and softened. Yetunde held his hand. Hannah sat at the foot of the bed. Two weeks later Yetunde wrote a letter to the hospice. She did not thank Hannah for the medication. She thanked her for the sentence about the closing door.

annotations.meaningVi: trung tâm chăm sóc cuối đời
bị dùng thuốc / mê man do thuốc

annotations.phrase: drown in the last six hours

annotations.meaningVi: cánh cửa đang khép (quyết định không đảo ngược)
cuộc sống nửa vời trên ghế (không còn thực sự sống)

grammarNotes.title: Used to + modal speculation for past habits and inferences
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng 'used to + V' để nói thói quen cũ. Trong bài: 'He used to say he wanted to die awake.' = trước đây ông hay nói vậy (nay không còn). Cũng có modal nói khả năng: 'he will probably keep breathing like that' (probable future); 'I would tell you if he were comfortable' (second conditional — giả thiết trái thực tế); 'I think he will not speak to you again either way' (will + not = dự đoán mạnh).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses 'used to + V' for a past habit. In the text: 'He used to say he wanted to die awake.' = he often said it in the past (no longer). Modals for likelihood appear too: 'he will probably keep breathing like that' (probable future); 'I would tell you if he were comfortable' (second conditional — contrary-to-fact); 'I think he will not speak to you again either way' (will + not = strong prediction).

translationVi:
Ông Olusegun Akin tám mươi tư. Ông ở một trung tâm cuối đời tại Manchester. Ông đã ngừng ăn và không còn hoàn toàn tỉnh. Đội ngũ đã đề nghị gia đình có thể dùng một loại an thần mạnh hơn để giúp ông thở dễ hơn qua đêm. Con gái ông, Yetunde, từ London đến. Y tá đêm Hannah đứng ở quầy. Vừa qua nửa đêm.

Yetunde: Tôi không muốn liều mạnh hơn. Cả đời ông từ chối thuốc giảm đau. Nếu ông thức dậy lúc ba giờ sáng và thấy mình bị mê thuốc, ông sẽ tức. Ông hay nói ông muốn chết khi còn tỉnh.

Hannah: Tôi hiểu. Nhưng đêm nay hơi thở của bố không như hôm qua. Nó không đều. Có lúc ông phải dùng cơ cổ để kéo không khí vào. Việc đó vất vả. Nếu chúng ta không làm gì thêm, có lẽ ông sẽ thở như vậy đến sáng. Ông sẽ không dễ chịu.

Yetunde: Chị chắc ông không dễ chịu? Trông ông bình thản.

Hannah: Trông bình thản vì ông quá mệt để biểu lộ điều khác. Cơ thể có cách trông yên khi đã hết lựa chọn. Tôi đã chăm cỡ một trăm người qua đêm cuối. Tôi sẽ nói chị biết nếu ông dễ chịu. Ông không dễ chịu.

Yetunde: Nhưng thuốc sẽ kéo ông xa tôi hơn. Hai ngày nay ông không nói với tôi. Nếu bị thuốc, ông sẽ không nói chút nào.

Hannah: Tôi nghĩ dù sao ông cũng sẽ không nói lại với chị. Xin lỗi vì nói thẳng. Không phải thuốc đang đưa ông đi xa.

Yetunde: Vậy chị đang nói tôi bắt ông chịu đau vì tôi.

Hannah: Tôi không nói vậy. Chính chị tự nói với mình, và tôi xin nói nhẹ là điều đó không công bằng với chị. Chị từ London lên. Chị ngồi với ông. Đó là điều ngược lại của ích kỷ. Nhưng tôi nghĩ đáng để hỏi: dễ chịu này là vì ai.

Yetunde: Ông luôn nói ông muốn chết khi còn tỉnh.

Hannah: Ông nói vậy khi còn khoẻ. Theo kinh nghiệm, những người nói vậy hầu hết ý là 'tôi không muốn bị thuốc ép thêm mười năm sống nửa vời trên ghế'. Họ không muốn nói 'tôi muốn ngạt thở trong sáu giờ cuối'. Theo tôi, đó là câu khác.

Yetunde: Cho tôi một phút.

Hannah: Tất nhiên. Tôi không đi đâu. Dù chị quyết gì, tôi cũng không đánh giá chị. Và — điều này quan trọng — chị có thể đổi ý theo cả hai chiều. Nếu tôi tiêm và hai mươi phút sau chị hối tiếc, hãy nói, tôi dừng. Lựa chọn này không phải cánh cửa đang khép lại.

Yetunde: Cho ông một liều nhỏ. Không phải liều đầy đủ. Và ở lại trong phòng với chúng tôi.

Hannah: Tôi sẽ.

Ông Akin mất lúc bốn giờ năm phút. Hơi thở chậm lại và dịu. Yetunde nắm tay ông. Hannah ngồi ở cuối giường. Hai tuần sau, Yetunde viết một lá thư cho trung tâm. Cô không cảm ơn Hannah vì thuốc. Cô cảm ơn vì câu nói về cánh cửa đang khép lại.

questions.prompt: Where and when does this conversation happen?
options: A Manchester hospice, shortly after midnight, with night nurse Hannah and daughter Yetunde from London
A Manchester home in the afternoon
A care home in the morning

questions.prompt: Why does Yetunde initially refuse the stronger sedative?
options: Her father had spent his life refusing painkillers and used to say he wanted to die awake

questions.prompt: What does Hannah observe about Mr Akin's breathing tonight?
options: Irregular; he is using neck muscles to pull in air — hard work

questions.prompt: Why does Hannah think he looks calm?
options: Too tired to look anything else; the body looks peaceful when it has run out of options
questions.explanation: Hannah's 'tired to look anything else' line.

questions.prompt: Why does Hannah think the drug isn't what is taking him away?
options: He likely won't speak to her again either way — the dying process, not the drug, is doing it
She refuses to give the drug

questions.prompt: How does Hannah respond to 'I am asking him to suffer for me'?
options: You are telling yourself that; it is unfair to you; but it's worth asking whose comfort the choice is for
Most people who say it mean 'I don't want ten more years of half-life in a chair' — not 'I want to drown in the last six hours'
She thinks he didn't mean it
She thinks he meant it literally

questions.prompt: What reassurance does Hannah give about the decision?
options: Yetunde can change her mind in either direction — the choice is not a closing door
A small dose, not the full one; Hannah to stay in the room
Dies at five past four; breathing slowed and softened; Yetunde held his hand

questions.prompt: What did Yetunde thank Hannah for, two weeks later?
options: Not the medication, but the sentence about the closing door
End-of-life choices are not only about medical effect but about whose comfort the choice is for — and the gift of a good carer is sometimes a sentence, not a substance.
Family should never argue with nurses.

cloze.template:
Narrator: Mr Olusegun Akin is eighty-four. He is in a {{b1}} in Manchester. He has stopped eating and is no longer fully conscious. The hospice team has offered the family the option of a stronger {{b2}} to ease his breathing through the night. His daughter, Yetunde, has come from London. The night nurse, Hannah, is at the desk. It is shortly after midnight.
Yetunde: I don't want the stronger sedative. He has spent his whole life refusing painkillers. If he wakes up at three in the morning and finds himself drugged he will be angry. He used to say he wanted to die {{b3}}.
Hannah: I hear that. But your father's breathing tonight is not what it was yesterday. It is {{b4}}. He is sometimes pulling in air with the muscles in his neck. That is hard work. If we do nothing extra, he will probably keep breathing like that until morning. He will not be comfortable.
Yetunde: Are you sure he isn't comfortable? He looks calm.
Hannah: He looks calm because he is too tired to look anything else. The body has a way of looking peaceful when it has run out of {{b5}}. I have nursed about a hundred people through their last night. I would tell you if he were comfortable. He isn't.
Yetunde: But the drug will take him further away from me. He has not spoken to me for two days. If he is drugged, he won't speak to me at all.
Hannah: I think he will not speak to you again either way. I am sorry to be direct. It is not the {{b6}} that is taking him away.
Yetunde: So you are telling me I am asking him to {{b7}} for me.
Hannah: I am not telling you that. You are telling yourself that, and I would gently say it is unfair to you. You came up from London. You are sitting with him. That is the opposite of selfish. But I do think it is worth asking whose {{b8}} the choice is for.
Yetunde: He always said he wanted to die awake.
Hannah: He said that when he was well. People who have said that have, in my experience, almost always meant 'I don't want to be drugged into ten more years of half-life in a chair.' He didn't mean 'I want to drown in the last six hours.' If you ask me, that is a different {{b9}}.
Yetunde: I will need a minute.
Hannah: Of course. I am not going anywhere. Whatever you decide, I will not think less of you. And — this matters — you can change your mind in either direction. If I give the dose and you regret it in twenty minutes, you can tell me, and I can stop. The choice is not a closing {{b10}}.
Yetunde: Give him a small dose. Not the full one. And stay in the room with us.
Hannah: I will.
Narrator: Mr Akin died at five past four. His breathing slowed and softened. Yetunde held his hand. Hannah sat at the foot of the bed. Two weeks later Yetunde wrote a letter to the hospice. She did not thank Hannah for the medication. She thanked her for the {{b11}} about the closing door.

blanks.explanation: The sentence about the closing door.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Hannah asks Yetunde 'whose comfort the choice is for.' Think of a small caring decision in your own family (visiting hours, food someone won't eat, a phone call). Was the comfort you were trying to protect actually theirs, or yours?
